does: This paper proposes Diffusion Nash Preference Optimization (Diff.-NPO), a game-theoretic framework for aligning text-to-image diffusion models with human preferences. Rather than relying on explicit reward models or the Bradley-Terry preference assumption, the method formulates alignment as finding a Nash equilibrium where the diffusion policy plays against itself for self-improvement. The approach eliminates the need for reward modeling while offering a more general treatment of preference signals beyond standard pairwise comparisons. Empirical validation on text-to-image generation demonstrates effectiveness across multiple metrics.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of RLHF research that has successfully aligned large language and vision models with human preferences. It directly extends Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which removed the need for explicit reward modeling by working directly with preference pairs in a computationally efficient manner. The paper responds to limitations in existing preference-based diffusion alignment methods that depend on the Bradley-Terry model's assumptions about preference structure. It connects to game-theoretic approaches in machine learning and Nash equilibrium concepts that have been applied to multi-agent and competitive learning scenarios, adapting these ideas to the single-agent preference alignment setting through self-play mechanisms.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward more flexible and general preference alignment approaches that can accommodate preference structures beyond pairwise comparisons. The game-theoretic perspective could enable future work on handling complex, multi-objective, or even contradictory human preferences more robustly than existing methods. The self-play mechanism provides a foundation for developing preference alignment without human feedback at inference time, potentially reducing computational costs in deployment. The approach may generalize beyond diffusion models to other generative systems, and could support research into preference aggregation across diverse human raters and exploration of equilibria in preference-based learning with non-standard preference models.
